name: Capital Allocation
description: The single most important function of a CEO: directing resources toward highest-return opportunities. Most value creation and destruction happens here.
examples: Berkshire's continuous reallocation. Jio's $30B network bet. Buffett's insurance float as perpetual low-cost capital.
counterExamples: GE Capital under Welch — financial leverage masking industrial decay. Kingfisher Airlines debt-funded expansion.

name: Ecosystem Lock-in
description: Users face high switching costs not because of the core product but because of the interconnected web of complementary services, data, and network effects around it.
examples: Apple: iPhone + App Store + iCloud + AirPods + Watch. Salesforce: CRM + integrations + AppExchange.
counterExamples: BlackBerry OS had no app ecosystem — switching cost was hardware, easily overcome.

name: Nokia
description: Finnish multinational that dominated global mobile phone markets in early 2000s before collapsing under iOS/Android disruption. Peak market cap ~$110B in 2000.
revenueEngine: Hardware device sales. Symbian licensing. Network infrastructure (separate division).
flywheel: Operator distribution -> volume scale -> component cost advantage -> price competitiveness -> more operator deals. Worked until platform mattered more than hardware.
switchingCosts: Low consumer switching costs. App ecosystem switching costs near zero on Symbian.
failurePoints: Symbian too old and fragile. No developer ecosystem. Hardware engineers dominated; software treated as secondary.

name: Kodak
description: American photography giant. Dominated film and camera markets for a century. Invented the digital camera in 1975 but failed to commercialize it. Filed Chapter 11 in 2012.
revenueEngine: Film sales (razor/blade — cameras priced low, film at 60%+ gross margin). Chemical processing.
flywheel: Film manufacturing scale -> cost leadership -> retail distribution -> consumer brand trust -> film repurchase. Destroyed by digital.
failurePoints: Business model entirely dependent on consumable film. Digital photography eliminated the recurring revenue stream.

name: Reliance Jio
description: Reliance Industries' telecom arm. Launched with free data in 2016, permanently disrupted Indian telecom economics. Built $30B+ network infrastructure.
revenueEngine: Mobile subscriptions. JioCinema, JioFiber. Ancillary digital services.
flywheel: Cheapest data -> massive user base -> content/services platform -> monetize at scale -> fund further infrastructure.
switchingCosts: Growing: JioCinema (IPL rights), JioFiber bundling, digital payments integration.

name: Nokia: Platform Collapse
summary: Nokia, holding 40% global handset market share, failed to recognize the iPhone as a platform shift. Its response — Symbian upgrades — was structurally incapable of competing with iOS/Android ecosystems.
rootCause:
1. Symbian unmaintainable (millions of lines of C++, different variants per handset).
2. Internal silos: software engineers had no power vs hardware.
3. Incentive structures rewarded hardware volume, not platform adoption.
warningSignsIgnored: RIM gaining enterprise share through software. iPhone dismissed as 'too expensive.' Android ecosystem growing at 10x Symbian rate. Internal written warnings about Symbian's ceiling (2005-2007).
lessons:
1. Platform beats product — evaluate the ecosystem, not just the device.
2. Software capability cannot be acquired quickly.
3. Fear of cannibalization kills faster than external competition.

name: Kodak: Invented It, Couldn't Use It
summary: Kodak engineer Steve Sasson invented the digital camera in 1975. The company shelved it for 20 years to protect film margins. Filed Chapter 11 in 2012.
rootCause:
1. Film was 60%+ gross margin — no manager wanted to cannibalize it.
2. Razor-blade model created structural dependency on consumables.
3. No software culture — Kodak was a chemistry and manufacturing company.
warningSignsIgnored: Own internal digital camera project (1975). Japan consumer electronics moving into digital through 1990s. Film volume declining year-on-year from 1999.
lessons:
1. Owning the patent on the future does not mean you'll profit from it.
2. Margin protection = innovation suppression.
3. Structural solutions needed — cultural change alone fails.

name: Netflix vs Blockbuster
summary: Netflix built a subscription DVD-by-mail model eliminating late fees (Blockbuster's key revenue line and biggest customer grievance). When streaming became viable, Netflix proactively cannibalized its own DVD business.
rootCause: Blockbuster's failure: late fees were 16% of revenue — structurally impossible to eliminate. Store footprint (9,000+ locations) was a liability in streaming era.
warningSignsIgnored: By Blockbuster: Netflix subscriber growth 2002-2005 was unambiguous. Consumer surveys consistently cited late fees as #1 pain point.
lessons:
1. Willingness to cannibalize yourself is the most powerful strategic weapon.
2. The incumbent's strength (physical stores) became its prison.
3. Subscription models that eliminate friction displace transaction models.

name: Jio: Weaponizing Capital
summary: Reliance invested $30B+ building India's only pan-India 4G LTE network before launching commercially. Launched with 6 months of free data in 2016. Within 18 months, three incumbents exited.
rootCause: Patient capital from Mukesh Ambani. Greenfield network on latest technology — no legacy costs. Priced at marginal cost to acquire user base. Government spectrum policy created an entry window.
warningSignsIgnored: By incumbents: Reliance's spectrum acquisitions 2012–2016 clearly signaled intent. Announced $20B investment publicly. Incumbents assumed it would be another Reliance Communications failure.
lessons:
1. Capital as competitive weapon — incumbents cannot match greenfield economics.
2. Platform logic: cheap data -> digital services monetization -> the real business.
3. Regulatory windows are temporary — move when they open.

name: WeWork: Governance Catastrophe
summary: WeWork raised $12.8B at valuations up to $47B by claiming tech platform economics. The 2019 IPO S-1 exposed: $47B in lease liabilities vs $2.4B revenue, Neumann extracting $700M via secondaries.
rootCause:
1. SoftBank Vision Fund with $100B+ enabled ego-driven capital deployment with no governance.
2. Supervoting shares (20:1) — Neumann had absolute control.
3. 'Tech premium' on real estate requires demonstrated network effects — WeWork had none.
warningSignsIgnored: 'Community-adjusted EBITDA' invented to exclude actual costs. Neumann trademarked 'We' and licensed it back to WeWork for $5.9M. Lease obligations growing 3x faster than revenue.
lessons:
1. Supervoting share structures enable founder extraction at shareholder expense.
2. Invented financial metrics almost always conceal something.
3. 'Tech premium' requires demonstrated network effects or data advantage.
